topics.title: Spring Batch
topics.description: Spring Batch is a framework for processing large volumes of data in bulk — CSV/XML imports, database migrations, report generation, payroll processing. It structures work into Jobs (the entire process) → Steps (units of work) → ItemReader / ItemProcessor / ItemWriter (the chunk-oriented processing pipeline). Built-in features: retry logic, skip policies, restartability from the last checkpoint, and parallel step execution.
topics.story: Imagine a postal warehouse processing 1 million parcels overnight. You can't handle them one by one with a customer service agent — you need an assembly line. Spring Batch is that assembly line. The Job is the night shift. Each Step is a conveyor belt station — one station weighs parcels (Reader), another stamps them (Processor), another loads them into trucks (Writer). If the power cuts out at 3am, the assembly line remembers exactly which parcel it was on — it restarts from checkpoint, not from parcel 1.
topics.analogy: Job = the entire batch run (e.g. 'Monthly Invoice Generation'). Step = one phase of the job (e.g. 'Read CSV → Filter → Write DB'). Chunk = batch size (process 100 items per transaction). ItemReader = reads one item at a time (CSV line, DB row). ItemProcessor = transforms/filters one item. ItemWriter = writes a chunk at a time (bulk insert). JobRepository = stores job execution state in DB. JobLauncher = triggers the job. JobParameters = runtime arguments (e.g. file path, date). Restartability = failed job picks up from last committed chunk.
topics.memoryAnchor: @EnableBatchProcessing — enables Spring Batch. @Bean Job — defines the job. @Bean Step — defines a step. StepBuilderFactory.get('step').chunk(100) — chunk-oriented step. FlatFileItemReader — reads CSV. JdbcBatchItemWriter — bulk DB writes. ItemProcessor<I,O> — one-at-a-time transform. @JobScope / @StepScope — late binding of job/step parameters. JobParametersBuilder — builds parameters. jobLauncher.run(job, params) — trigger programmatically. JobExecutionListener — onBefore/AfterJob hooks.
topics.firstPrinciples: Chunk-oriented processing: Spring Batch reads N items one-at-a-time (ItemReader), processes each (ItemProcessor), then writes the entire chunk in one transaction (ItemWriter). On failure within a chunk, the transaction rolls back and only that chunk is retried (not the whole file). A JobRepository persists step execution state (STARTED, COMPLETED, FAILED, step count) so restarts resume from the last successful chunk. Tasklet steps handle non-chunk work (delete temp files, send notification email). Partitioned steps split data across multiple threads or JVMs for parallel processing.
topics.bruteForce: Manual batch: while ((line = reader.readLine()) != null) { process(line); db.insert(result); } — no transaction boundary, no restart capability, single-threaded, no skip/retry logic. One error in record 50,000 means starting over from record 1.
topics.optimizedApproach: Spring Batch with chunk size 500: reads 500 CSV lines, processes each, writes 500 rows in a single bulk INSERT. If row 423 fails (invalid data), skip policy skips it, logs it to an error file, and continues. If the JVM crashes at chunk 80, restart reads chunk 81 — skipping the 80 already committed chunks.
topics.whenToUse: Nightly ETL (Extract, Transform, Load) pipelines. Database migration scripts (old schema → new schema). Monthly billing/invoice generation. Import large CSV/Excel files uploaded by users. Batch report generation. NOT for: real-time/low-latency operations (use Kafka consumers), small one-time scripts (use CommandLineRunner), streaming data (use Spring Integration).

examples.title: CSV to Database Batch Job
examples.explanation: FlatFileItemReader reads one CSV line per call. ItemProcessor filters/transforms (returns null to skip). JdbcBatchItemWriter does bulk INSERT for each chunk. chunk(100) commits every 100 items — on failure, only that chunk rolls back. faultTolerant().skip() skips bad CSV lines (up to skipLimit). Unique JobParameters (timestamp) allow the same job to run multiple times. The JobRepository in the DB tracks each run's status.
examples.realWorldUse: Monthly billing system: reads all active subscriptions from DB (JdbcPagingItemReader), calculates invoice amounts (ItemProcessor), writes to invoices table and queues email notifications (CompositeItemWriter). Runs at 2am via @Scheduled. If it fails at invoice 8,000 of 50,000, restart picks up at 8,001 — no duplicate charges.

problems.title: Chunk-Oriented Pipeline Trace
problems.description:
Given N items and a chunk size C, simulate the chunk-oriented processing pipeline:
- Items are integers 1 to N
- Processor: doubles each item
- Writer: prints all items in a chunk as comma-separated on one line, prefixed with `Chunk:`

Input: first line is N, second line is C
Output: each chunk on one line

Sample input:
```
7
3
```
Expected:
```
Chunk: 2,4,6
Chunk: 8,10,12
Chunk: 14
```
problems.hint1: Read N items one at a time (loop i=1 to N). Process each: doubled = i*2.
problems.hint2: Accumulate into a List<Integer>. When size == chunkSize (or loop ends), print the chunk and clear the list.
problems.hint3: Use String.join(",", list.stream().map(String::valueOf).collect(...)) to format the chunk.
problems.editorial: This simulates Spring Batch's chunk-oriented model: read one item, process it, accumulate N items, then write the whole chunk at once (bulk transaction). The last chunk may be smaller than chunkSize — it still gets written. This buffering pattern is why Spring Batch's JDBC writer uses executeBatch() for efficiency.

problems.title: Skip Policy Simulation
problems.description:
Simulate Spring Batch's skip policy. Process N integers. For each:
- If the number is divisible by 7, it is 'invalid' — skip it (do NOT process)
- Otherwise, add it to a running total

Print `Total: X` and `Skipped: Y`.

Sample input: `1 7 14 3 21 5`
Expected:
```
Total: 9
Skipped: 3
```
problems.hint1: Iterate through numbers. Check n % 7 == 0 for skip condition.
problems.hint2: Maintain two counters: total (sum of non-skipped) and skipped (count of divisible-by-7 items).
problems.hint3: Print both results at the end.
problems.editorial: Spring Batch's skip policy lets you configure specific exception types and a skip limit. When a record causes a known exception (e.g., DataIntegrityViolationException), it's skipped and logged rather than failing the whole job. This simulates the logic: ItemProcessor returns null (or throws a skippable exception) for invalid items.

problems.title: Restart Simulation — Resume from Checkpoint
problems.description:
Simulate batch restartability. You process records 1 to N in chunks of size C. A 'previous run' completed K chunks before failing.

On restart, skip the first K*C records (already processed) and process the rest.

Input: N, C, K on first line.
Output: each remaining chunk as `Chunk X: items` (X is the chunk number starting from K+1).

Sample input: `10 3 2`
(N=10, C=3, K=2 completed chunks — so skip records 1-6)

Expected:
```
Chunk 3: 7,8,9
Chunk 4: 10
```
problems.hint1: Calculate startIndex = K * C + 1. Begin processing from startIndex.
problems.hint2: Chunk number starts at K+1 and increments as chunks are filled.
problems.hint3: Collect items in a chunk of size C; when full (or loop ends), print the chunk.
problems.editorial: Spring Batch's JobRepository persists the last successfully committed step execution, including the read count. On restart, Spring Batch skips already-processed items by calling ItemReader.setCurrentItemCount() or using JdbcPagingItemReader with OFFSET. The key insight: only committed chunks are 'done' — a failed chunk means those items will be re-processed.
